coredata.dc:title: An innovative red-emitting nickel-doped carbon dot for simultaneous detection of Fe(III) and pyrophosphate ions, coupled with synergistic photothermal/photodynamic cancer therapy

Zhang, Wenqian

Fang, Yi
Xiao, Qi
coredata.dc:description:
Background Multifunctional carbon dots (CDs) have gained prominence in biosensing, cell imaging, and nanomedicine due to their tunable fluorescence, excellent biocompatibility, and surface functionalizability. However, blue/green-emitting CDs suffer from limited tissue penetration and autofluorescence interference. Iron ions (Fe3+) and pyrophosphate ions (PPi) are crucial biomarkers in various biological processes, with deviations in their concentrations linked to various diseases including cancer. Therefore, the development of highly sensitive and selective sensors for Fe3+ and PPi detection is vital. To address these challenges, we present red-emitting nickel-doped carbon dots (Ni-CDs) synthesized via a simple one-pot hydrothermal method, offering advantages for deep-tissue imaging and therapeutics.
Results Herein, we report a one-pot hydrothermal synthesis of nickel-doped CDs (Ni-CDs) that exhibit both paramagnetic responsiveness and red fluorescence (λ ex/λ em = 475 nm/630 nm). Using citric acid and p-phenylenediamine as raw materials, we successfully fabricated Ni-CDs with high fluorescence quantum yield of 24 % and uniform size distribution (5.13 ± 0.04 nm). The Ni-CDs serve as an "on-off-on" fluorescent sensor for simultaneous detection of Fe3+ and PPi with low detection limits of 0.051 μM and 0.31 μM, respectively. They demonstrate high sensitivity and selectivity in detecting Fe3+ in real water samples and PPi in human urine and blood samples. Furthermore, Ni-CDs exhibit good biocompatibility and enable real-time visualization of intracellular Fe3+ and PPi dynamics in cancer cells. Under 808 nm laser irradiation (1.5 W/cm2), Ni-CDs achieve high photothermal conversion efficiency of 59.1 % and generate cytotoxic reactive oxygen species, leading to synergistic photothermal/photodynamic cancer cell apoptosis of 98.8 %.
Significance This "two-in-one" theranostic platform based on Ni-CDs overcomes traditional limitations of conventional blue/green-emitting CDs, offering deep-tissue compatibility and minimized background interference, and multifunctional integration. Our methodology provides a generalized design strategy for wavelength-engineered CD hybrids, enabling innovative applications in point-of-care diagnostics and precision oncology. The multifunctional properties of Ni-CDs highlight their potential to revolutionize biomedical research and clinical practice.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The photothermal properties of Ni-CDs were investigated using infrared thermography to monitor temperature changes across a range of concentrations (0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, and 0.5 mg/mL) and under varying laser intensities (0.3, 0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2 W/cm2). Temperature measurements were recorded at 30-s intervals over a 10-min period. To evaluate the photothermal stability of Ni-CDs, the sample was exposed to NIR laser irradiation, allowed to cool naturally to room temperature, and this cycle was repeated four times. Furthermore, the photothermal conversion efficiency of Ni-CDs under NIR laser irradiation (808 nm, 1.5 W/cm2) was calculated to provide a comprehensive analysis of their performance (see supporting information).This work was financially supported by Central Government Guided Local Science and Technology Development Fund Project (guikeZY22096017), Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (2025GXNSFAA069589, 2024GXNSFDA010036), and National Natural Science Foundation of China (22164014, 22463008, U23A2089).

This work was financially supported by Central Government Guided Local Science and Technology Development Fund Project ( guikeZY22096017 ), Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province ( 2025GXNSFAA069589 , 2024GXNSFDA010036 ), and National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22164014 , 22463008 , U23A2089 ).

Detection of Fe3+ ion in water samples and PPi in human serum and urine samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: An innovative red-emitting nickel-doped carbon dot for simultaneous detection of Fe(III) and pyrophosphate ions, coupled with synergistic photothermal/photodynamic cancer therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, Key Laboratory of Nanobiosensor Analysis, College of Chemistry and Materials, Nanning Normal University, Nanning, 530001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multifunctional carbon dots (CDs) have gained prominence in biosensing, cell imaging, and nanomedicine due to their tunable fluorescence, excellent biocompatibility, and surface functionalizability. However, blue/green-emitting CDs suffer from limited tissue penetration and autofluorescence interference. Iron ions (Fe3+) and pyrophosphate ions (PPi) are crucial biomarkers in various biological processes, with deviations in their concentrations linked to various diseases including cancer. Therefore, the development of highly sensitive and selective sensors for Fe3+ and PPi detection is vital. To address these challenges, we present red-emitting nickel-doped carbon dots (Ni-CDs) synthesized via a simple one-pot hydrothermal method, offering advantages for deep-tissue imaging and therapeutics.

Herein, we report a one-pot hydrothermal synthesis of nickel-doped CDs (Ni-CDs) that exhibit both paramagnetic responsiveness and red fluorescence (λ ex/λ em = 475 nm/630 nm). Using citric acid and p-phenylenediamine as raw materials, we successfully fabricated Ni-CDs with high fluorescence quantum yield of 24 % and uniform size distribution (5.13 ± 0.04 nm). The Ni-CDs serve as an "on-off-on" fluorescent sensor for simultaneous detection of Fe3+ and PPi with low detection limits of 0.051 μM and 0.31 μM, respectively. They demonstrate high sensitivity and selectivity in detecting Fe3+ in real water samples and PPi in human urine and blood samples. Furthermore, Ni-CDs exhibit good biocompatibility and enable real-time visualization of intracellular Fe3+ and PPi dynamics in cancer cells. Under 808 nm laser irradiation (1.5 W/cm2), Ni-CDs achieve high photothermal conversion efficiency of 59.1 % and generate cytotoxic reactive oxygen species, leading to synergistic photothermal/photodynamic cancer cell apoptosis of 98.8 %.

This "two-in-one" theranostic platform based on Ni-CDs overcomes traditional limitations of conventional blue/green-emitting CDs, offering deep-tissue compatibility and minimized background interference, and multifunctional integration. Our methodology provides a generalized design strategy for wavelength-engineered CD hybrids, enabling innovative applications in point-of-care diagnostics and precision oncology. The multifunctional properties of Ni-CDs highlight their potential to revolutionize biomedical research and clinical practice.

Schematic illustration of preparation of Ni-CDs, dual detection of Fe3+ and PPi ions (1), and synergistic photothermal/photodynamic anticancer effects (2).
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with particle sizes ranging from 0 to 10 nm [1], have revolutionized biomedical engineering since their serendipitous discovery in 2004 [2]. Their unique combination of tunable fluorescence, superior biocompatibility, and flexible surface functionalizability [3] has enabled applications spanning biosensing, bioimaging, and theranostics. While blue/green-emitting CDs dominate current research, their short emission wavelengths (<550 nm) suffer from intrinsic limitations: shallow tissue penetration (<2 mm), severe autofluorescence interference, and phototoxicity under prolonged UV light exposure. In contrast, red-emitting CDs (λ em > 600 nm) address these challenges by enabling deep-tissue imaging (>5 mm penetration) and minimizing photodamage [4], yet their synthesis remains constrained by complex doping strategies and low quantum yields (<15 %) [5–7].

Metal doping has emerged as a powerful strategy to engineer CDs' optoelectronic properties [8,9]. Metal ions can dramatically alter the optical, electronic, and magnetic properties of CDs by modifying electron density distribution and energy level differences. The integration of metal elements endows CDs with unique physicochemical properties, thereby broadening their application spectrum [10,11]. Transition metals, in particular, introduce localized surface plasmon resonance and paramagnetism while modulating electron transitions. Nickel doping, for instance, has significantly advanced the properties of CDs, leading to improvements in surface oxidation, fluorescence quantum yield (QY), and absorption range [12], and it offers distinct advantages over other metals in the synthesis of doped CDs. Specifically, nickel enhances the electronic transition capability of CDs, promotes the generation of oxygen radicals, and confers inherent paramagnetism, which collectively contribute to its enhanced photothermal and photodynamic properties while maintaining fluorescence through defect state engineering. These properties make nickel-doped CDs (Ni-CDs) highly promising for applications in photothermal therapy (PTT), photodynamic therapy (PDT), and as contrast agents in magnetic resonance imaging [13,14]. Additionally, nickel's superior biocompatibility and low toxicity ensure safe application in biological systems, its cost-effectiveness and abundance facilitate large-scale synthesis and widespread adoption, and its tunable optical and magnetic properties enable the development of multifunctional nanomaterials tailored for specific applications. Furthermore, nickel's potential to serve as contrast agents in magnetic resonance imaging and mediate synergistic anticancer effects through combined PTT and PDT therapies further underscores its advantages over other metals in the field of doped CD synthesis. Pioneering research conducted by Wu et al. demonstrated the solvothermal synthesis of red-emitting Ni-CDs using toxic N,N-diethylformamide, achieving a fluorescence QY of 18 % [14]. Subsequent studies by Tian [12] and Zhang [13] employed hydrothermal methods but yielded only green-emitting Ni-CDs with limited functionalities. Recognizing the advantages of the hydrothermal approach, including its simplicity, cost-effectiveness, scalability, and its ability to facilitate the homogeneous integration of nickel ions into the CD structure, we selected this method for our synthesis. This uniform doping is critial for endowing the Ni-CDs with the desired optical and magnetic properties, making the hydrothermal method particularly suitable for the large-scale production of Ni-CDs with consistent performance. However, despite these advancements, existing approaches suffer from critical shortcomings: (1) reliance on organic solvents generating hazardous waste, (2) multi-step purification requirements, and (3) inadequate exploration of therapeutic potential. Consequently, there is an unmet need for a paradigm shift towards the eco-friendly synthesis of multifunctional red-emitting Ni-CDs.

Therefore, exploring the synthesis of novel red-emitting Ni-CDs with superior optical properties using a simple and economical hydrothermal approach holds immense scientific significance and application value. The choice of starting materials for the synthesis of CDs is crucial as it directly influences their properties and performance. Citric acid (CA), a tricarboxylic acid, is rich in carboxyl groups, which can facilitate the formation of a carbonaceous framework through dehydration and carbonization processes during hydrothermal synthesis [15]. Its small molecular size and high water solubility also contribute to a homogeneous reaction mixture, promoting the uniform growth of CDs. On the other hand, p-phenylenediamine (p-PDA), an aromatic amine, serves as a nitrogen source and provides additional functional groups (such as amino groups) that can be doped into the carbon structure, enhancing the CDs' optical and electronic properties [16]. The combination of CA and p-PDA has been previously shown to produce CDs with high fluorescence quantum yields and excellent biocompatibility [16], making them ideal candidates for our study.

Metal ions and inorganic compounds play crucial roles in natural settings, biological entities, and chemical reactions. Iron ions (Fe3+), a vital metallic component, are involved in numerous physiological functions, including metabolic pathways, enzymatic activities, and the synthesis of hemoglobin [17]. However, deviations in Fe3+ concentrations can lead to severe physiological disturbances, causing conditions such as diabetes, renal dysfunction, iron-deficiency anemia, and even cancer [18,19]. Similarly, pyrophosphate ions (P2O7 4−, PPi) are ubiquitous anions within biological systems, playing pivotal roles in regulatory processes like energy transduction, intracellular ATP hydrolysis, and nucleotide polymerization [20]. Anomalies in PPi concentrations are also tightly linked to conditions like rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, and cancer progression, positioning them as potential biomarkers for cancer diagnosis [21,22]. Consequently, the development of highly sensitive and selective sensors for Fe3+ and PPi detection is imperative.

Traditional detection methods for Fe3+ and PPi, such as liquid chromatography and atomic absorption spectrometry, are often plagued by complexities in operation, prolonged detection times, and high costs, limiting their widespread application [23–26]. In contrast, fluorescence spectrometry, distinguished by its ease of use, non-invasive detection, and rapid response, has garnered considerable attention in sensing and imaging domains. The blue-emitting CDs-based "on-off-on" fluorescent sensing approach, in particular, offers remarkable advantages for the detection of metal ions and anions, owing to its simplicity, high sensitivity, and real-time monitoring capabilities [21,27]. However, the excitation wavelengths of these CDs induce cellular phototoxicity, limiting live-cell applications. Red-emitting CDs, with their ability to mitigate UV-induced photodamage and reduce background interference, provide a robust tool for the sensitive and selective quantification of these critical ions in diverse biological and environmental settings. Yet, currently, there exists less red-emitting Ni-CD-based system for dual Fe3+ and PPi sensing.

In recent years, PTT and PDT have emerged as promising local phototherapy approaches in cancer treatment, offering non-invasive alternatives that precisely target and damage tumor cells while alleviating hypoxia within the tumor microenvironment [28]. The combination of PTT and PDT not only exhibits remarkable synergistic therapeutic efficacy against tumors but also markedly diminishes the systemic toxicity linked to conventional treatments. CDs, with their nanoscale size, exceptional biocompatibility, ease of modification, and outstanding optical properties, show immense potential in biomedical applications [29]. Recent advances in transition metal doping suggest a solution: nickel's d-orbital electrons enhance nonradiative transitions for PTT while maintaining fluorescence through defect state engineering [29]. However, existing metal-doped CDs exhibit <47 % photothermal efficiency [28] and negligible singlet oxygen (1O2) generation, failing to harness combined PTT/PDT. Moreover, research on the synergistic anticancer effects of PTT and PDT mediated by magnetic Ni-CDs remains scarce.

Herein, we present a simple one-pot hydrothermal synthesis of red-emitting paramagnetic Ni-CDs using CA and p-PDA as raw materials, and nickel chloride hexahydrate as the dopant source. Utilizing a static quenching mechanism, we designed an "on-off-on" fluorescent sensor that effectively detects Fe3+ and PPi with strong biocompatibility in authentic samples and cellular environments. This method is simple, cost-effective, and highly sensitive, offering new perspectives on metal-doped red-emitting CD synthesis and biosensing. Furthermore, Ni-CDs exhibit high reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation and excellent photothermal conversion, making them promising photosensitizers for enhanced anticancer effects through synergistic PTT/PDT. This "two-in-one" multifunctional platform underscores Ni-CDs' potential in biochemical applications and paves the way for advancements in metal-doped CD development.

All chemical reagents, including CA, p-PDA, NiCl2·6H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, and PPi, were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Biological materials, such as human cervical cancer HeLa cells, human breast cancer MCF-7 cells, and human gastric mucosal epithelial GES-1 cells, were purchased from Shanghai Gening Biological Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM), 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazole-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrabromozole (MTT), fetal bovine serum, trypsin-EDTA solution, penicillin-streptomycin liquid, Calcein-AM, propidium iodide (PI), 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), singlet oxygen sensor green (SOSG), and the Annexin V-FITC/PI Apoptosis Detection Kit, along with other biological reagents, were purchased from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). The ultrapure water used in all experiments, with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm, was purified using a Millipore-Q ultrapure water system.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) data of the Ni-CDs were acquired using a JEM-2011 EFF field emission TEM instrument (JEOL, Japan). The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Thermo Fisher FT-IR spectrometer, while Raman spectra were collected with a LabRAM Odyssey Nano Raman spectrometer (HORIBA, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were performed on a Thermo Escalab 250xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). Fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired using an RF6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and a UV3600 plus spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), respectively. Time-resolved fluorescence spectra were obtained using a Horiba QM-8075 high-sensitivity steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba, Japan). Zeta potential measurements were performed on a SZ-100 nanoparticle size analyzer (HORIBA, Japan). Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra were obtained using a Bruker A300-10/12 EPR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) hysteresis curves were measured with a Quantum Design SQUID-VSM MPMS-3 vibrating sample magnetometer (Quantum Design, USA). Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was carried out on a Siemens Prisma 3.0 T MRI scanner (Siemens, Germany). Photothermal properties were evaluated using a VCL-808nm MO-3W laser (Beijing Honglan Optoelectronics Technology Co., Ltd, China) and a UTi320E device (Unidux Technology Co., Ltd, China). Apoptosis was quantified using a FACS CALIBUR flow cytometer (BD, USA). The pH of the solution was precisely adjusted with a Sartorius PB-10 pH meter (Sartorius, China). Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) images were captured using a state-of-the-art Leica confocal laser scanning microscope from Leica (Leica, Germany).

Initially, 0.02 g of p-PDA and 0.02 g of NiCl2·6H2O were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water. The mixture was then sonicated for 5 min to facilitate complete dissolution. Following this, 0.06 g of CA was introduced into the solution, and the mixture was sonicated for an additional 5 min. The pH of the resulting solution was measured and found to be 5.5. Subsequently, the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave reactor and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. After the reactor cooled to room temperature, the crude product was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to precipitate and remove any large particles. The supernatant was then filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to further purify the solution. The filtrate underwent dialysis for 24 h using a dialysis bag with a 500 Da molecular weight cutoff to remove any remaining impurities. Finally, the solid Ni-CDs were obtained by freeze-drying the contents of the dialysis bag, yielding the purified product.

The cytotoxicity of Ni-CDs toward HeLa, MCF-7, and GES-1 cells was assessed using the MTT assay. Initially, the cells were seeded in 96-well plates and cultured in DMEM within a 5 % CO2 incubator at 37 °C with saturated humidity for 24 h to allow for cell attachment and proliferation. Following this, varying concentrations of Ni-CDs were introduced into the wells, and the plates were incubated under identical conditions. After the incubation period, 200 μL of MTT solution (0.5 mg/mL) was added to each well, and the plates were incubated for an additional 4 h to facilitate the formation of purple formazan crystals. Subsequently, the medium was carefully aspirated, and the formazan crystals were dissolved in 200 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) by gentle agitation for 10 min. The optical density (OD) of the resultant solution was measured at a wavelength of 490 nm using an ELISA reader, with pure DMSO serving as the blank control to ensure accurate readings. In a separate experiment, HeLa and MCF-7 cells were cultured for 24 h before being co-incubated with different concentrations of Ni-CDs for 4 h. The cells were then thoroughly washed with 10 mM phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) at pH 7.4 to remove any unbound Ni-CDs. Following this, the cells were exposed to NIR light (808 nm, 1.5 W/cm2) for 10 min and incubated for an additional 24 h. Cell viability was calculated using the formula: viability (%) = (ODtreated/ODcontrol) × 100, where ODcontrol and ODtreated represent the OD values of cells without and with Ni-CDs, respectively. The results are presented as the mean ± standard deviation from three independent experiments to ensure reliability and reproducibility.

For CLSM imaging, HeLa and MCF-7 cells were seeded in 35 mm glass-bottom dishes at a density of 2 × 105 cells per well and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 and high humidity to promote cell adhesion and growth. The medium was then replaced with fresh medium containing 0.3 mg/mL Ni-CDs, and the cells were incubated for 4 h to allow for cellular uptake of the Ni-CDs. Prior to imaging, the cells were washed three times with 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4) to remove any extracellular Ni-CDs and medium. CLSM images were immediately captured at room temperature to visualize the intracellular distribution and localization of the Ni-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ and PPi
ce:section.ce:para: A solution of Ni-CDs was prepared at a concentration of 15 μg/mL in 50 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 4.5). To this solution, varying concentrations of Fe3+ were introduced, and the mixtures were allowed to react for 1 min at room temperature. Subsequently, different concentrations of PPi were added to a system containing 70 μM Fe3+ and 15 μg/mL Ni-CDs. The reactions were allowed to proceed for an additional 1 min at room temperature. The fluorescence spectra were measured using an excitation wavelength of 475 nm, and the emission intensity was recorded at 675 nm to assess the fluorescent response of Ni-CDs to the presence of Fe3+ and PPi.

For cellular detection, HeLa and MCF-7 cells were seeded in 35 mm glass-bottomed Petri dishes and cultured at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 environment with saturated humidity for 24 h to ensure proper cell attachment and growth. The cells were then incubated with 300 μg/mL of Ni-CDs for 6 h to allow for cellular uptake of the nanoparticles. After washing the cells three times with 50 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 4.5) to remove any extracellular Ni-CDs, 2 mL of the same buffer solution was added to the dishes for cell imaging. Various concentrations of Fe3+ were then introduced to the cells, and each addition was followed by a 5-min incubation period to allow for the interaction between Fe3+ and Ni-CDs within the cells. Cellular imaging was performed after each incubation period to observe the fluorescent response. Subsequently, the cells were supplemented with 2 mL of 50 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 4.5) containing 500 μM Fe3+, and different concentrations of PPi were added to the cells. Each addition of PPi was followed by an 8-min incubation period to allow for the interaction between PPi, Fe3+, and Ni-CDs within the cells. Cellular imaging was performed after each incubation period to monitor the changes in fluorescence intensity, which could indicate the presence and concentration of PPi within the cells.

Water samples were collected from various locations in Nanning City and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The resulting supernatant was then filtrated through 0.22 μm microporous membranes. For the human samples, both blood and urine were obtained from healthy adult volunteers in our laboratory, with informed consent secured for all experimentation involving human subjects. The protocols for these experiments were approved by the ethics committee of Nanning Normal University and conducted in accordance with relevant Chinese laws and institutional guidelines (WS/T 225–2002). Human urine samples underwent centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was filtrated through 0.22 μm microporous membranes before being diluted 50-fold with 50 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 4.5). For the blood samples, fresh samples were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 8 min, and 1 mL of the supernatant was mixed with 15 mL of perchloric acid. After stirring for 5 min, the mixture was centrifuged again at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and the resulting supernatant was diluted 100-fold using 50 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 4.5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal and photodynamic effects of Ni-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The photothermal properties of Ni-CDs were investigated using infrared thermography to monitor temperature changes across a range of concentrations (0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, and 0.5 mg/mL) and under varying laser intensities (0.3, 0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2 W/cm2). Temperature measurements were recorded at 30-s intervals over a 10-min period. To evaluate the photothermal stability of Ni-CDs, the sample was exposed to NIR laser irradiation, allowed to cool naturally to room temperature, and this cycle was repeated four times. Furthermore, the photothermal conversion efficiency of Ni-CDs under NIR laser irradiation (808 nm, 1.5 W/cm2) was calculated to provide a comprehensive analysis of their performance (see supporting information).

For the assessment of photodynamic effects, the generation of ROS was quantified using 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein (DCFH). Initially, DCFH was obtained by reacting DCFH-DA with NaOH, followed by neutralization with 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4). Subsequently, DCFH was added to both Ni-CDs and PBS solutions, which were then exposed to 808 nm laser irradiation for 6 min. The fluorescence intensity at 525 nm was monitored every minute to quantify ROS generation. The 1O2 generation was detected using SOSG. SOSG was dispersed in both Ni-CDs and PBS solution, and the mixtures were irradiated with 808 nm laser light. The fluorescence intensity at 525 nm were regularly measured to track 1O2 production.

Additionally, EPR technology was employed, with 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine as the spin trap, to further confirm 1O2 generation. Changes in the EPR spectrum were observed both in the dark and under 808 nm laser irradiation. To visualize ROS generation within living cancer cells, MCF-7 cells were seeded in a culture dish, treated with varying concentrations of Ni-CDs, and stained with DCFH-DA following NIR laser irradiation. The generation of ROS within the cells was then visualized using a laser confocal microscopy, providing insights into the photodynamic effects of Ni-CDs at the cellular level.

To investigate the combined photothermal and photodynamic effects of Ni-CDs on cancer cells, MCF-7 cells were initially plated at a density of 3 × 105 cells per well in 35 mm glass-bottom dishes and cultured for 24 h to ensure proper cell attachment and growth. Following this, the cells were exposed to 0.3 mg/mL of Ni-CDs for 4 h to facilitate cellular uptake of the nanoparticels. After thorough washing with PBS to remove any extracellular Ni-CDs, the cells were subjected to an 808 nm NIR laser at 1.5 W/cm2 for 8 min to induce photothermal and photodynamic effects. Subsequently, the cells were stained with calcein-AM and PI to distinguish between live and dead cells. Calcein-AM stains live cells with green fluorescence, while PI stains dead cells with red fluorescence. The stained cells were then imaged using laser confocal microscopy to visually assess the combined photothermal/photodynamic effect of Ni-CDs on cell viability.

Furthermore, flow cytometry was employed to evaluate cell apoptosis following PTT/PDT. For this analysis, MCF-7 cells were plated at the same density in 6-well plates and incubated for 24 h. The cells were then treated with 0.3 mg/mL of Ni-CDs for 4 h, followed by NIR light irradiation (808 nm, 1.5 W/cm2) for 8 min to induce apoptosis. After laser treatment, the cells were stained using the Annexin V-FITC/PI Apoptosis Detection Kit, which allows for the differentiation of apoptotic cells from viable and necrotic cells based on their fluorescence properties. The stained cells were analyzed using a FACS CALIBUR flow cytometer (BD, USA) to quantify the percentage of apoptotic cells. This analysis provides valuable insights into the anticancer effect of the Ni-CDs-based PTT/PDT, helping to elucidate the mechanisms underlying the observed cell death and potential therapeutic applications of such Ni-CDs.

All experimental data were measured in triplicate and results were presented as mean ± standard deviation. Statistical analysis was performed using one-way ANOVA with GraphPad Prism 9 software, while data graphs were created with Origin 9 software.

In the field of CDs synthesis, it has been well-established that precise adjustments to reactant ratios, heat treatment conditions, and the incorporation of specific doping elements can profoundly influence the structural and optical characteristics of CDs [16,30]. For Ni-CDs, precise control over synthesis conditions is paramount, as it not only dictates their structural integrity but also directly impacts their potential applications in biosensing, bioimaging, and combined PTT/PDT. As illustrated in Fig. S1, we conducted a systematic investigation into the effects of varying the quantities of p-PDA, CA, and NiCl2·6H2O, as well as the temperature and duration of hydrothermal treatment, on the synthesis of Ni-CDs. Through rigorous experimental optimization, we identified an optimal set of synthesis conditions: 0.02 g of p-PDA, 0.06 g of CA, 0.02 g of NiCl2·6H2O, and hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 6 h. Following these steps, the pH of the mixture was measured to be 5.5. This pH value can be attributed to the inherent properties of the dissolved compounds and their interactions in the aqueous solution. CA, being a weak organic acid, undergoes partial dissociation in water, releasing H+ ions and thereby lowering the pH of the solution. The presence of p-PDA and NiCl2, along with their specific concentrations, likely influenced the overall pH through their own dissociation or complexation reactions with CA or water molecules. The resulting pH of 5.5 was deemed appropriate for our synthesis process, as it provided a stable and homogeneous solution conducive to the subsequent hydrothermal reaction. This pH value also ensured that the starting materials remained in a soluble form, facilitating their reaction and the formation of Ni-CDs with the desired properties. Ni-CDs synthesized under these conditions not only exhibit a stable structure but also demonstrate exceptional optical and magnetic performances, making them highly promising candidates for various biomedical applications.

As depicted in Fig. 1 a, Ni-CDs exhibit a nearly spherical shape and demonstrate excellent dispersibility in aqueous solution. To provide a more comprehensive understanding of their nanostructure, we have included TEM images at various magnifications (Fig. S2), accompanied by selected area electron diffraction patterns and lattice fringe images. The high-resolution TEM image (inset in Fig. 1a) further elucidates the crystalline structure of Ni-CDs, revealing a lattice spacing of approximately 0.32 nm, which aligns closely with the (002) plane spacing of graphene (0.3354 nm). This similarity underscores the high degree of carbonization within the Ni-CDs [31–33], contributing to their structural stability and serving as the cornerstone for their remarkable optical and magnetic properties. To gain deeper insights into the particle size distribution, we employed the Gaussian distribution law for particle size analysis. Upon analyzing over 500 particles of varying sizes, it is observed that the majority of Ni-CDs exhibit a nearly spherical shape and a uniform size distribution ranging from 2.5 to 9.5 nm, with an average size of 5.13 ± 0.04 nm (Fig. 1b). However, a few larger particles are also present in the sample, likely due to factors such as aggregation or slight variations in reaction conditions during synthesis. The zeta potential of the Ni-CDs, measured at −12.6 mV, indicates a moderate level of colloidal stability, with the presence of larger particles potentially attributed to subtle differences in surface chemistry or charge distribution compared to the smaller, more uniformly sized particles [34]. Nonetheless, the overall colloidal stability and performance of the Ni-CDs are likely unaffected by the presence of these larger particles, given the excellent dispersion and uniform size distribution observed for the majority of particles.

The XRD pattern of the Ni-CDs offers compelling evidence of their graphite carbon structure, as depicted in Fig. 1c. The distinct peaks observed at 2θ = 23.1° and 28.5° corresponded to the characteristic diffraction peaks of the graphite carbon (002) plane [35]. Additionally, a prominent peak at 2θ = 38.2° aligns with the characteristic diffraction peak of the graphitic carbon (100) plane [36]. These observations underscore the high crystallinity of the Ni-CDs. To further investigate the graphitization degree of the Ni-CDs, Raman spectroscopy was employed (Fig. S3). The peaks observed at 1372 cm−1 and 1538 cm−1 correspond to the D-band (sp3 hybridization) and G-band (sp2 hybridization), respectively. The intensity ratio of the D-band to G-band (I D/I G = 0.94) is indicative of a high density of surface defects on the CDs, which is consistent with a high degree of graphitization on the surface of the Ni-CDs [37]. This finding provides additional insights into the carbon structure and the effect of nickel doping on the Ni-CDs. In conjunction with the findings from TEM, XRD, and Raman spectroscopy, we can confidently affirm the presence of a graphite carbon structure within the Ni-CDs. This structural feature is crucial for tailoring their optical and electrical properties, rendering them highly suitable for a range of bio-related applications.

FT-IR spectroscopy was employed to delve deeper into the chemical bonds and functional groups present in Ni-CDs. Fig. 1d reveals the absorption peaks at 3324.54 cm−1 and 3201.22 cm−1, which are indicative of the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H bonds [38]. This suggests that Ni-CDs are endowed with hydrophilic functional groups, notably hydroxyl and amino groups, contributing to their excellent water solubility and thereby enhancing their suitability for bioapplications. Furthermore, the spectrum displays absorption peaks at 1628.74 cm−1 and 1509.27 cm−1, attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO, N–H, and CC bonds [39]. Additional peaks at 1403.74 cm−1, 1311.03 cm−1, and 1250.36 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of C–O–H, C–C, and C–N bonds, respectively [37,40]. The absorption peaks observed at 1162.24 cm−1 and 1085.44 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibrations of the aliphatic C–O bond [41], while the peak at 828.71 cm−1 is attributed to aromatic N–H vibrations [42]. These findings not only corroborate the presence of diverse functional groups on the surface of Ni-CDs but also underscore their significance in determining the material's water solubility.

To gain a comprehensive understanding of the surface chemical composition of Ni-CDs, XPS analysis was conducted. Fig. 1e displays the XPS full spectrum of Ni-CDs, unequivocally showcasing the presence of C, N, O, and Ni elements. Their respective characteristic binding energy peaks are distinctly observed at 284.98 eV (C 1s), 399.49 eV (N 1s), 532.00 eV (O 1s), and 855.63 eV (Ni 2p), serving as a pivotal basis for subsequent detailed examination. Quantitative analysis further elucidated the elemental composition, with carbon being the predominant element at approximately 70.29 %, nitrogen at about 16.6 % indicating significant doping within the carbon lattice to enhance optical and electronic properties (Fig. S4). The oxygen content was measured at around 8.97 %, likely attributed to surface functional groups improving solubility and biocompatibility. Lastly, the nickel content was confirmed to be approximately 4.14 %, confirming successful integration and imparting unique functionalities such as altered fluorescence and enhanced catalytic activity. Additionally, EDS spectra and elemental mapping (Fig. S5) corroborated the presence of these elements with weight percentages of 83.77 % C, 8.73 % N, 4.68 % O, and 2.93 % Ni, respectively.

Furthermore, high-resolution XPS spectra were utilized to conduct an exhaustive analysis of the surface chemical bonding states of Ni-CDs. Fig. 1f presents the high-resolution C 1s spectrum, which reveals three prominent peaks at 284.78 eV, 285.83 eV, and 287.39 eV. These peaks correspond to C–C/CC bonds, C–N/C–O bonds, and CO bonds, respectively [43,44]. This detailed examination offers valuable insights into the structural composition of the Ni-CDs, revealing the presence of various carbon bonding states that contribute to their overall electronic structure. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of N 1s displays three prominent peaks at 398.78 eV, 399.41 eV, and 400.92 eV (Fig. 1g), respectively. These peaks indicate the presence of imines (=N-), amines (-NH2), and protonated amines (-NH3 +) groups on the Ni-CDs surface. The presence of these nitrogen-containing functional groups not only enriches the surface chemical structure but also influences the electronic properties of the Ni-CDs by altering the electron density distribution and introducing additional electronic states [45]. In the high-resolution XPS analysis of O 1s (Fig. 1h), two peaks at 531.23 eV and 532.68 eV are attributed to CO bonds and C–OH/C–O–C bonds, respectively. These peaks provide direct evidence for the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on the Ni-CDs surface, which further contribute to the modification of the electronic structure by introducing additional electronic states and affecting the charge distribution within the material [46]. Most notably, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Ni 2p (Fig. 1i) displays four characteristic peaks at 855.09 eV, 861.07 eV, 872.38 eV, and 880.46 eV, respectively. The peaks at 855.09 eV and 872.38 eV correspond to the Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 energy levels, respectively, while the peaks at 861.07 eV and 880.46 eV are typical satellite peaks [47]. The binding energy shifts observed in the Ni 2p spectrum are particularly significant, as they directly reflect the chemical state of nickel within the Ni-CDs and its interaction with the surrounding carbon lattice. These shifts indicate changes in the electron density around the nickel atoms, which in turn influence the overall electronic structure of the Ni-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and magnetic properties of Ni-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of Ni-CDs were comprehensively investigated through UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 2 a illustrates the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Ni-CDs, exhibiting two prominent peaks at 230 nm and 278 nm. These peaks are attributed to the π → π∗ transitions of CC bonds and aromatic sp2 structures, respectively [48]. The additional absorption peaks observed at 359 nm and 516 nm originate from the n → π∗ transitions associated with CO surface state and sp2 hybridized cores, respectively [49,50]. The fluorescence spectrum of Ni-CDs reveals that the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths are centered around 475 nm and 630 nm, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 2b, upon increasing the excitation wavelength from 440 nm to 475 nm, a substantial enhancement in the fluorescence intensity of Ni-CDs is observed. However, a further increase in the excitation wavelength, ranging from 475 nm to 550 nm, results in a notable decrease in fluorescence intensity, while the emission wavelength remains unaffected. This observation suggests that the emission characteristic of Ni-CDs are independent of the excitation wavelength, which could be correlated with the uniform particle size, excellent dispersion, and favorable surface states of Ni-CDs, as confirmed by TEM images [51]. Fig. 2c showcases the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Ni-CDs, which extends from the UV region into the NIR region, covering the range from 600 nm to 1000 nm. The gradual increase in absorbance with escalating Ni-CDs concentration indicates their tunable photothermal performance within the NIR-I region, thereby laying the foundation for their utilization in photothermal therapy.

As illustrated in Fig. 2d, the average fluorescence lifetime of Ni-CDs was determined to be approximately 1.51 ns through biexponential fitting (see supporting information, Fig. S6). The Ni-CDs exhibited an absolute fluorescence QY of 24 %, as assessed using an integrating sphere system (see supporting information), which is sufficiently high for potential in vivo optical imaging applications. Fig. 2e demonstrates that the emission intensity of Ni-CDs remained nearly constant after being stored for 30 days, indicating their excellent fluorescence stability. To further evaluate their optical stability, Ni-CDs were subjected to extreme experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. S7, the fluorescence intensity of Ni-CDs remained virtually unaltered in a high ionic strength environment of 2 M NaCl and after 120 min of UV irradiation at 365 nm, and the fluorescence intensity of Ni-CDs remained stable across a wide pH range of 4–10. These results suggest that the synthesized Ni-CDs exhibit outstanding optical stability, which are highly suitable for analytical detection applications.

Owing to the integration of Ni elements, the Ni-CDs are anticipated to exhibit magnetic properties. The magnetic hysteresis curve corresponding to the Ni-CDs is depicted in Fig. S8. The VSM hysteresis loop reveals that the Ni-CDs display paramagnetic behavior [8], characterized by a magnetization of 0.25 emu/g and a coercivity of 36.47 Oe. Notably, these values surpass those of other CDs doped with transition metal ions such as Mn, Co, and Fe [8,40]. The enhanced paramagnetic behavior of Ni-CDs can be attributed to the effective magnetization arising from the high Ni element content within the Ni-CDs structure. The excellent paramagnetic properties of the Ni-CDs underscore their potential as efficient weighted magnetic resonance contrast agents for MRI application. Theoretically, Ni2+ possesses two unpaired 3d electrons, suggesting its efficacy as a T1-weighted MRI contrast agent [12]. To investigate this, we assessed the longitudinal relaxation (r 1) and transverse relaxation (r 2) rates of the Ni-CDs. Our findings indicate that both the T1-weighted and T2-weighted MRI signals increased linearly with the Ni content in the Ni-CDs (Fig. 2f and Fig. S9). Linear fitting of the data with 1/T1, 1/T2, and Ni concentration revealed that the r 1 relaxation rate of the Ni-CDs was 0.87 mM−1, while the r 2 relaxation rate was 0.63 mM−1. The relatively high r 1 relaxation rate of the Ni-CDs suggests their suitability as a T1-weighted positive contrast agent in MRI applications. As illustrated in Fig. 2f, an apparent gradual enhancement in the brightness of T1-weighted MRI was observed with an increase in Ni concentration from 0.05 mM to 1.0 mM. Collectively, these results predict that Ni-CDs hold promise as a potent T1 contrast agent in MRI applications and as a tumor diagnostic probe for both fluorescence imaging and MRI dual-mode imaging in the field of biomedical imaging [8,12].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection principle of Fe3+ and PPi
ce:section.ce:para: When Fe3+ and PPi were gradually introduced to the Ni-CDs, an intriguing "on-off-on" triple-state emission behavior was observed. It is widely recognized that quenching mechanisms encompass static quenching, dynamic quenching, resonance energy transfer, photoinduced electron transfer, and inner filter effects [6]. To gain deeper insights into the mechanism underlying the Fe3+-induced quenching of Ni-CDs, we meticulously UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence lifetime experiments. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, the UV–vis absorption peak of Fe3+ ion is centered around 300 nm, whereas the Ni-CDs exhibit absorption peaks at approximately 230 nm, 278 nm, and 516 nm. Upon the addition of Fe3+ ion to the Ni-CDs, a notable blue shift from about 513 nm to 495 nm is observed in the UV–vis absorption peak of both the Ni-CDs + Fe3+ and Ni-CDs/Fe3+ systems, accompanied by a decrease in the absorbance of Ni-CDs. Moreover, the non-overlapping UV–vis absorption peaks of the Ni-CDs + Fe3+ and Ni-CDs/Fe3+ systems indicate the formation of a ground-state complex between the Ni-CDs and the Fe3+ ion [51]. Interestingly, as shown in Fig. 3b, when 70 μM PPi was added to the Ni-CDs/Fe3+ system, the Ni-CDs/Fe3+/PPi assembly exhibited a reversal in its absorption peak, shifting it back to 516 nm. This shift can be attributed to the chelation between the Fe3+ ion and PPi [52]. Furthermore, the absorption peak of the Ni-CDs/Fe3+/PPi system at 516 nm is significantly lower than that of the Ni-CDs alone at the same wavelength. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the Ni-CDs/PPi and Ni-CDs + PPi systems are identical, while the Fe3+ + PPi system exhibits higher absorbance compared to the Fe3+/PPi system. These findings suggest that the n → π∗ electronic transition of the Ni-CDs is partially absorbed by the PPi-Fe3+ complex.

Furthermore, as illustrated in Fig. 3c, there is no overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Fe3+ ion and the excitation and emission spectra of the Ni-CDs, thus ruling out the possibility of a fluorescence inner filter effect [7]. In terms of fluorescence lifetime, Fig. 3d and e reveal that the Ni-CDs exhibit an average fluorescence lifetime of (1.44 ± 0.02) ns. Upon the addition of varying concentrations of Fe3+ ions and PPi to the Ni-CDs solution, the average fluorescence lifetime of the Ni-CDs remains approximately 1.45 ns, with no discernible changes observed. This indicates that there is no energy transfer occurring between the Ni-CDs and the Fe3+ ions [7,53]. These results strongly suggest that the quenching mechanism of Fe3+ ions on the fluorescence of Ni-CDs is primarily static quenching. The "on-off" fluorescence quenching of Ni-CDs upon the addition of Fe3+ can be attributed to several factors. Firstly, Fe3+ ions possess a high charge density and can form strong electrostatic interactions with the functional groups present on the surface of Ni-CDs. These interactions can lead to the formation of non-fluorescent complexes between Ni-CDs and Fe3+, thereby quenching the fluorescence of Ni-CDs. Secondly, Fe3+ions can also induce electron transfer processes between Ni-CDs and Fe3+, which can further contribute to the fluorescence quenching. The subsequent "on" fluorescence recovery upon the addition of PPi can be explained by the strong binding affinity between PPi and Fe3+. PPi can effectively chelate Fe3+ ions, forming stable PPi-Fe3+ complexes. This chelation process reduces the concentration of free Fe3+ ions available to interact with Ni-CDs, thereby disrupting the non-fluorescent complexes formed between Ni-CDs and Fe3+. As a result, the fluorescence of Ni-CDs is recovered. In addition to these electrostatic and chelation interactions, the specific structural and electronic properties of Ni-CDs also play a crucial role in the "on-off-on" fluorescence sensing mechanism. The presence of nickel dopants in Ni-CDs can modulate their electronic structure and surface chemistry, making them more susceptible to interactions with Fe3+ and PPi. Furthermore, the size, shape, and surface functionalization of Ni-CDs can also influence their interaction with Fe3+ and PPi, thereby affecting the fluorescence behavior. These findings highlight the significant potential of Ni-CDs as a versatile fluorescent sensor for the detection of both Fe3+ and PPi, highlighting their applicability in various sensing and diagnostic applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous detection of Fe3+ and PPi
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve optimal analytical detection performance, we carried out a comprehensive optimization of the detection conditions. As depicted in Fig. S10, for the detection of Fe3+ ion, the fluorescence signal, represented by the ratio I 0/I (where I 0 and I denote the fluorescence intensities of Ni-CDs at 630 nm before and after the addition of Fe3+ ion, respectively), reached its peak when the concentration of Ni-CDs was set at 15 μg/mL, the pH of the reaction system was adjusted to 4.5, and the reaction time was 1 min. Similarly, for the detection of PPi, the fluorescence signal I/I 0 peaked at a Fe3+ concentration of 70 μM and a reaction time of 1 min.

Under these optimized experimental conditions, we evaluated the sensing performance of Ni-CDs towards Fe3+ ion. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a, an increase in the concentration of Fe3+ ions led to a gradual decrease in the fluorescence intensity of Ni-CDs at approximately 630 nm. Fig. 4b shows that the I 0/I ratio steadily increased as the Fe3+ ion concentration rose from 0 to 200 μM. A robust linear relationship was observed between the Fe3+ ion concentration in the range of 0.1–70 μM and the I 0/I ratio, following the linear regression equation I 0/I = 0.0238 × [Fe3+] (μM) + 0.999, with a high correlation coefficient of 0.992. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated using the formula LOD = 3σ/S, where σ represents the standard deviation of 11 blank measurements and S is the slope of the linear regression equation. The calculated LOD was 0.051 μM, which is significantly lower than the maximum permissible Fe3+ ion content in drinking water specified by the National Health Commission of the People's Republic of China (GB 5749-2022) of 0.3 mg/L. Furthermore, our method offers a comparable or even lower LOD than other CD-based fluorescent techniques, with a satisfactory linear range for Fe3+ ion detection (Table S1). These findings emphasize the sensitivity and reliability of our Ni-CDs-based fluorescent detection method for Fe3+ ion. In addition, as the Fe3+ ion concentration increased from 0 to 200 μM, the solution's color gradually lightened (Fig. 4c), ultimately turning black-red. Under 365 nm UV light irradiation, the fluorescence color of the solution progressively faded from red until it completely disappeared. This result demonstrates that Ni-CDs can be utilized not only for the spectrophotometric analysis and detection of Fe3+ ion but also through visual inspection, highlighting their versatility and practicality in various detection applications.

To evaluate the specificity of the Ni-CDs sensor for Fe3+ ion detection, we systematically investigated the impact of various common cations on Ni-CDs fluorescence quenching and the fluorescence recovery upon the addition of biomolecules and anions. Under optimized Fe3+ detection conditions (Fig. S11a), cations like Ba2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cu2+, K+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Ag+, Na+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, and Fe2+ were introduced to the Ni-CDs solution. The results revealed that, except for 70 μM Fe3+ ion, none of these cations significantly altered the Ni-CDs fluorescence, indicating high selectivity for Fe3+. To further test anti-interference, all the mentioned ions along with 70 μM Fe3+ were added to the Ni-CDs solution (Fig. S11b). Minimal changes in fluorescence intensity were observed, confirming the sensor's excellent anti-interference ability and its reliability for Fe3+ detection in real samples.

As illustrated in Fig. 4d, under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence intensity of Ni-CDs at approximately 630 nm in the Ni-CDs/Fe3+ system gradually recovered with an increase in PPi concentration. This trend is further elucidated in Fig. 4e, where the fluorescence signal ratio I'/I 0' (where I 0' and I′ represent the fluorescence intensity values of the Ni-CDs/Fe3+ system at 630 nm before and after the addition of PPi, respectively) continuously increased as the PPi concentration rose from 0 to 100 μM. Notably, within the 0.5–40 μM range, a strong linear correlation was observed between the PPi concentration and the I'/I 0' ratio, following the linear regression equation I'/I 0' = 0.00747 × [PPi] (μM) + 1.03, with LOD of 0.31 μM and a correlation coefficient of 0.992. Our Ni-CDs fluorescent probe offers a competitive linear range and a lower detection limit compared to other methods (Table S2). Furthermore, the solution's color progressively changed from dark red to lighter hues with an increase in PPi concentration from 0 to 100 μM (Fig. 4f). Under 365 nm UV light irradiation, the emitted light gradually returned, providing a clear visual response to PPi. These findings confirm that the Ni-CDs/Fe3+ system is a versatile method for PPi analysis, suitable for both visual inspection and fluorescence spectroscopy. With its high sensitivity, excellent linearity, and visual detection ability, this system is an attractive candidate for PPi detection in various applications.

Fig. S11c shows the Ni-CDs/Fe3+ system has remarkable PPi detection capabilities. Except for 0.1 mM of PPi, the I 0'/I′ ratio remained unaffected by substances like HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, SO4 2−, HSO4 −, Cl−, Br−, F−, NO3 −, NO2 −, urea, chitosan, glucose, alanine, cysteine, glutathione, and ascorbic acid, demonstrating high selectivity for PPi detection. Fig. S11d indicates minimal changes in the I 0'/I′ ratio with 50 mM of interfering substances and 0.1 mM of PPi, confirming high specificity and robustness against interference for PPi detection, outperforming some reported methods and other CDs-based techniques, and achieving a lower PPi detection limit.

To validate practicality and versatility of our sensor, spiked recovery experiments were done on real water, human serum, and human urine samples to quantify Fe3+ and PPi concentrations. As detailed in Table 1 , the recoveries range from 96.2 % to 104.7 % with relative standard deviations consistently below 6.6 %. These findings strongly suggest that our "on-off-on" fluorescence sensing platform is highly effective for detecting Fe3+ ion in complex environmental matrices and PPi in biological samples, thereby validating the practical utility of our fluorescent probe in real-world applications.

In a comprehensive investigation using the MTT assay (Fig. S12), we assessed the cellular toxicity of Ni-CDs. HeLa, MCF-7, and GES-1 cells showed survival rates above 80 % when incubated with Ni-CDs concentrations ranging from 0 to 300 μg/mL for 24 and 48 h. This indicates that Ni-CDs have minimal cellular toxicity and excellent biocompatibility with live cells. Additionally, CLSM was used to examine the impact of Ni-CDs on cell morphology and their potential for live cell imaging. After incubating HeLa and MCF-7 cells with 200 μg/mL of Ni-CDs at 37 °C for 4 h (Fig. S13), cell morphology and structure remained unchanged, while distinct red fluorescence was observed intracellularly, particularly in the nuclei, suggesting Ni-CDs' potential for live cell nuclear imaging [53]. These findings highlight Ni-CDs as a promising high-performance fluorescent probe for live cell bioanalysis and imaging.

To further investigate the potential of Ni-CDs as a precise and sensitive monitoring tool, we introduced Fe3+ ion and PPi into living MCF-7 cells, replicating the dynamic ionic environment found within cellular compartments. To ensure the accuracy and reliabililty of our results, we performed three washes using 50 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 4.5) to remove any unbound ions and extraneous materials. As clearly depicted in Fig. 5 a, Ni-CDs emitted a stable and intense red fluorescence within the cells, solidifying their reliablity as a robust indicator for subsequent ion detection efforts. Upon gradually increasing the Fe3+ concentration, we observed a marked, dose-dependent decrease in the red fluorescence intensity of Ni-CDs, underscoring the exceptional sensitivity of Ni-CDs in dynamically monitoring intracellular Fe3+ levels. Furthermore, as the PPi concentration increased, there was a notable recovery in the red fluorescence intensity of Ni-CDs (Fig. 5b). This fluorescence resurgence further confirmed to the sensitivity and specificity of the Ni-CDs/Fe3+ system in detecting PPi. By comparing the fluorescence recovery at various PPi concentrations, we observed a gradual increase in the red fluorescence intensity of Ni-CDs, eventually surpassing the fluorescence emission level of the control sample. This finding unequivocally demonstrates the promising application of Ni-CDs in the dynamic monitoring of intracellular Fe3+ ion and PPi levels, further solidifying their potential as a versatile and powerful tool in biological research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal and photodynamic properties of Ni-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Photothermal property of Ni-CDs was investigated firstly. Leveraging the extensive light absorption capabilities of Ni-CDs across both the visible and infrared spectra, we assessed their photothermal efficacy by exposing various concentrations of Ni-CDs solutions to an 808 nm NIR laser (1.5 W/cm2) for 600 s. As illustrated in Fig. 6 a, a noticeable color shift in the thermal images corresponds to a temperature change that increases with the concentration of Ni-CDs [54]. Fig. 6b reveals that after 10 min of 808 nm NIR laser (1.5 W/cm2) irradiation, the temperature of the PBS solution rose only to 31.7 °C. In contrast, the final temperatures of Ni-CDs solutions at concentrations of 10 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL, 100 μg/mL, 200 μg/mL, 300 μg/mL, and 500 μg/mL reached 43.8 °C, 54.7 °C, 58.7 °C, 66.3 °C, and 77.2 °C, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 6c, the temperature of a 300 μg/mL Ni-CDs solution increased from 47.7 °C to 74.6 °C as the 808 NIR laser power was elevated from 0.3 W/cm2 to 2 W/cm2, highlighting the outstanding photothermal properties of Ni-CDs and their ability to effectively convert laser energy into heat. Furthermore, the temperature profile remained highly stable over four laser switching cycles (Fig. 6d), demonstrating the robust and consistent photothermal conversion behavior of the synthesized Ni-CDs. Based on the photothermal heating-cooling curve presented in Fig. S14, the photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of Ni-CDs in water under 808 nm NIR laser (1.5 W/cm2) irradiation was calculated (see supporting information) [55], resulting in a value of 59.1 % (Fig. 6e). This efficiency exceeds that of previously reported CD systems used for PTT under similar conditions (Table S3). Collectively, these findings emphasize the promising potential of Ni-CDs for PTT application, owing to their exceptional photothermal performance and stability.

The Photodynamic property of Ni-CDs was investigated firstly. The effectiveness of Ni-CDs in generating ROS under 808 nm NIR laser (1.5 W/cm2) irradiation was assessed using DCFH-DA as a sensitive fluorescent indicator [56]. As evident from Fig. S15, Ni-CDs demonstrated a significant increase in fluorescence intensity compared to the PBS solution, indicating their high efficiency in ROS generation and underscoring their potential PDT application. Furthermore, SOSG, a well-recognized probe for detecting 1O2, was employed to evaluate the Ni-CDs' ability to generate 1O2 [57]. Upon reaction with 1O2, SOSG forms an endoperoxide (SOSG-EP) that emits strong fluorescence at 525 nm [57]. As illustrated in Fig. S15, Ni-CDs significantly enhanced the fluorescence intensity at 525 nm compared to the PBS solution, confirming their high efficiency in generating 1O2. This result also verifies that the ROS produced by Ni-CDs is indeed in the form of 1O2. Further depicted in Fig. 6f, under 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine trapping, the characteristic triplet signal peaks with a 1:1:1 ratio confirmed the generation of 1O2 upon 808 nm NIR laser (1.5 W/cm2) irradiation, further confirming their ability to generate 1O2 [58].

To further investigate the production of intracellular ROS, MCF-7 cells were pretreated with DCFH-DA probes. These probes are oxidized by ROS, resulting in the formation of dichlorofluorescein, which emits green fluorescence. As depicted in Fig. S16, the 808 nm NIR laser (1.5 W/cm2) irradiated group exhibited a noticeable bright green fluorescence that intensified with increasing Ni-CDs concentration. This observation confirms that Ni-CDs can effectively generate ROS within MCF-7 cells under NIR laser irradiation.

The MTT assay was employed to assess the in vitro anticancer efficacy of Ni-CDs on HeLa and MCF-7 cells under 808 nm NIR laser (1.5 W/cm2) irradiation. As shown in Fig. 7 a and b, the induction of cell death was found to be concentration-dependent. Initially, when the concentration of Ni-CDs reached 300 μg/mL without laser irradiation, the survival rates of both HeLa and MCF-7 cells remained above 90 %. However, when these cells were exposed to the 808 nm NIR laser (1.5 W/cm2) in the presence of 300 μg/mL Ni-CDs, the survival rates significantly decreased to approximately 9 % and 8 % for HeLa and MCF-7 cells, respectively. These results not only underscore the biocompatibility of Ni-CDs under normal conditions but also highlight their exceptional anticancer effects when combined with NIR laser irradiation.

Live/dead cell staining was employed to evaluate the impact of PTT/PDT on apoptosis in MCF-7 cells within a controlled in vitro setting [59]. The MCF-7 cells were exposed to four different treatment conditions: (i) Control (PBS), (ii) Control (PBS) with 808 nm NIR laser (1.5 W/cm2) (+), (iii) Ni-CDs alone, and (iv) Ni-CDs combined with 808 nm NIR laser (1.5 W/cm2) (+). Following these treatments, the MCF-7 cells were stained with Calcein-AM and PI for visualization purposes [54,59]. The cells in the control group (PBS) exhibited a bright green fluorescence, indicating high cell viability (Fig. 7c). Similarly, the MCF-7 cells in both the control (PBS) + NIR (+) and Ni-CDs-only groups also demonstrated strong green fluorescence. This suggests that neither 808 nm NIR laser (1.5 W/cm2) irradiation alone nor Ni-CDs alone had a detrimental effect on the tumor cells, as there was no induction of red fluorescence (indicative of dead cells) or a decrease in green fluorescence (indicative of cellular damage). However, when examined under a fluorescence microscope, the group treated with both Ni-CDs and 808 nm NIR laser (1.5 W/cm2) [Ni-CDs + NIR (+)] displayed a prominent red signal. This observation highlights the significant photothermal and photodynamic killing effect of Ni-CDs, effectively inducing cell apoptosis. The red signal indicates that the cells have lost their membrane integrity, allowing PI to enter and bind to nucleic acids, which is a characteristic feature of dead or dying cells.

To further elucidate the cell apoptosis mechanism, flow cytometry was used to analyze cells stained with Annexin V-FITC/PI after undergoing combined photothermal and photodynamic treatment [54,59]. As depicted in Fig. 7d, the majority of cells (exceeding 99.0 %) in the group without 808 nm NIR laser (1.5 W/cm2) irradiation were concentrated in the lower left quadrant, indicating their good survival status following the aforementioned treatments. In contrast, after 808 nm NIR laser (1.5 W/cm2) exposure, the apoptosis rate in cells incubated with Ni-CDs surged to 98.8 %. This high apoptosis rate is attributed to the synergistic photothermal and photodynamic destruction of tumor cells by Ni-CDs. The flow cytometry data confirmed that Ni-CDs-treated cells suffered irreversible damage following combined PTT and PDT, leading to effective tumor cell ablation. The Annexin V-FITC/PI staining allows for the differentiation between early apoptotic, late apoptotic, and necrotic cells, providing a comprehensive understanding of the cell death mechanism induced by Ni-CDs under 808 nm NIR laser (1.5 W/cm2) irradiation. Collectively, these findings demonstrate that Ni-CDs can significantly enhance both PTT and PDT when combined with NIR laser irradiation, leading to the destruction of cancer cells. The synergistic effect of photothermal and photodynamic processes showcases the significant potential of Ni-CDs for application in PTT/PDT cancer therapy, offering a promising approach for the treatment of cancer.

In our study, we demonstrate that Ni-CDs act as a dual-modal therapeutic agent by integrating PDT and PTT to achieve synergistic anticancer efficacy. PDT harnesses light-activated generation of ROS to induce oxidative cellular damage, while PTT converts optical energy into localized heat to ablate tumor cells. The unique properties of Ni-CDs, stemming from their surface states, nickel doping, and strong photothermal conversion efficiency under near-infrared irradiation, enable simultaneous activation of both mechanisms: their photosensitizing capacity facilitates ROS production for PDT, while their efficient light-to-heat conversion drives PTT. This synergy is further amplified by two interrelated mechanisms: ROS generated during PDT enhances cellular membrane permeability, priming cancer cells for heightened thermal sensitivity during PTT; concurrently, PTT-induced hyperthermia increases tumor blood flow and oxygen perfusion, which in turn elevates ROS generation efficiency during PDT. Collectively, these reciprocal interactions between PDT and PTT, mediated by Ni-CDs' integrated optical and thermal properties, create a cooperative therapeutic outcome where combined oxidative stress and thermal ablation surpass the individual contributions of either modality alone.

Herein, we successfully synthesized high-yield Ni-CDs through a simple one-pot hydrothermal process and devised an "on-off-on" fluorescence sensing method for detecting Fe3+ and PPi with high sensitivity. The probe shows a wide detection range and low limits (0.051 μM for Fe3+, 0.31 μM for PPi), with high sensitivity and recovery rates in real samples. The paramagnetic properties of Ni-CDs make them suitable as T1-weighted MRI contrast agents. Ni-CDs also enable visual detection in living cells and exhibit strong NIR absorption, achieving a photothermal conversion efficiency of 59.1 % and generating ROS for effective PTT/PDT cancer treatment. This "two-in-one" multifunctional Ni-CDs platform holds great potential in biosensing and cancer treatment, advancing carbon-based nanomaterials and paving the way for innovative biomedical research and cancer therapy.
